title: Tate Locus - Conjectures and Results
speaker: Anna Gwenaelle Cadoret
abstract: Let $k$ be a field and $X$ a geometrically connected variety over $k$. The Tate or degeneracy locus of a $l$-adic local system on $X$ is the etale counterpart of the Hodge locus of a VHS. While in the last decade tremendous progresses have been made in understanding the latter thanks to, in particular, techniques from o-minimality, much less is known about the former. I will review the main conjectures (and mention briefly some applications) about this locus when k is a number field, and explain what we can currently prove. If time remains, I will sketch some of the proofs. This should include joint works with Jakob Stix and Akio Tamagawa.
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: From motivic special loci to the Tate locus
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up varieties over a number field, recalls motivic special loci coming from exceptional cycles, and translates the relevant l-adic realization into a representation-theoretic definition of the Tate locus.

proof_stages.label: Uniform open image conjecture for bounded-degree arithmetic
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the main arithmetic problem by truncating points by residue-degree and states the uniform open image conjecture: bounded-degree Tate-exceptional points should be sparse, while outside the Tate locus the index of the local image in the generic image should be uniformly bounded.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and range of the conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The conjecture is motivated by its implications for motivic loci, jumping Néron-Severi ranks, and uniform torsion phenomena, while the speaker emphasizes that current methods and even the plausible formulation are much safer in dimension one or for rational points.

proof_stages.label: Toric and trivial strata inside the Tate locus
proof_stages.summary: To obtain more accessible problems, the Tate locus is stratified by the structure of the local monodromy group, especially the toric locus and the trivial locus, and corresponding finiteness or emptiness conjectures are stated, including level-cover reformulations.

proof_stages.label: Conjectural mechanisms behind the toric and trivial predictions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the proposed finiteness statements are expected to follow from broader conjectures such as weak Lang/level conjectures, generalized André-Oort, and unramified Fontaine-type statements, and sketches the p-adic Hodge-theoretic route from a trivial point to potential unramifiedness.

proof_stages.label: Curve-case proof via Diophantine towers and growing genus
proof_stages.summary: For the uniform open image conjecture in the case dim X=1, the problem is reformulated in terms of rational points on level towers, then reduced via Diophantine finiteness to proving that the genera of the covering curves go to infinity; the higher-dimensional analogue is stated as a level conjecture predicting general type.

proof_stages.label: Positive-density toric-locus theorem
proof_stages.summary: For weakly motivic compatible systems satisfying Hodge-theoretic and level hypotheses, the speaker states a partial toric-locus result over a positive-density set of primes and outlines the proof: extend the system across a good compactification, prove integrality of toric points, and apply a Lawrence-Venkatesh method at carefully chosen ordinary places.

proof_stages.label: Trivial-locus theorem using rationality, companions, and reduction
proof_stages.summary: The final result concerns the trivial locus: under rationality or compatible-system hypotheses one obtains the expected finiteness/triviality conclusion, with proof ingredients including p-to-l companions, potential stable reduction to curves, extension to integral models, and passage to the special fiber.

examples.name: Jumping Néron-Severi rank in fibers
examples.note: The speaker notes that the sparsity part of the conjecture would imply bounded-degree sparsity for fibers whose Néron-Severi rank jumps.
examples.engagement_reason: Jumping of the Néron-Severi rank is cited as a consequence/application of the conjecture, with no verification on particular fibers.
